question:
Let $d$ be a real number. For each integer $m \geq 0$, define a
sequence $\{a_m(j)\}$, $j=0,1,2,\dots$ by the condition
\begin{align*}
a_m(0) &= d/2^m, \\
a_m(j+1) &= (a_m(j))^2 + 2a_m(j), \qquad j \geq 0.
\end{align*}
Evaluate $\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n(n)$.
solution:
Solution. We have \( a_{m}(j+1)+1=\left(a_{m}(j)+1\right)^{2} \), so by induction on \( j \),
\[
a_{m}(j)+1=\left(a_{m}(0)+1\right)^{2^{j}} .
\]

Hence
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} a_{n}(n)=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{d}{2^{n}}\right)^{2^{n}}-1
\]

If \( f(x)=\ln (1+d x) \), then \( f^{\prime}(x)=d /(1+d x) \), and in particular
\[
\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\ln (1+d x)}{x}=f^{\prime}(0)=d
\]

Applying the continuous function \( e^{x} \) yields
\[
\lim _{x \rightarrow 0}(1+d x)^{1 / x}=e^{d}
\]
and taking the sequence \( x_{n}=1 / 2^{n} \) yields
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{d}{2^{n}}\right)^{2^{n}}=e^{d}
\]
so \( \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} a_{n}(n)=e^{d}-1 \).

descriptive_long.question:
Let $realparam$ be a real number. For each integer $indexlevel \geq 0$, define a
sequence $\{sequencelevel(iteration)\}$, $iteration=0,1,2,\dots$ by the condition
\begin{align*}
sequencelevel(0) &= realparam/2^{indexlevel}, \\
sequencelevel(iteration+1) &= (sequencelevel(iteration))^2 + 2sequencelevel(iteration), \qquad iteration \geq 0.
\end{align*}
Evaluate $\lim_{n \to \infty} sequencelimit(n)$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We have \( sequencelevel(iteration+1)+1=\left(sequencelevel(iteration)+1\right)^{2} \), so by induction on \( iteration \),
\[
sequencelevel(iteration)+1=\left(sequencelevel(0)+1\right)^{2^{iteration}} .
\]

Hence
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} sequencelimit(n)=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{realparam}{2^{n}}\right)^{2^{n}}-1
\]

If \( logfunction(variable)=\ln (1+realparam\ variable) \), then \( logfunction^{\prime}(variable)=realparam /(1+realparam\ variable) \), and in particular
\[
\lim _{variable \rightarrow 0} \frac{\ln (1+realparam\ variable)}{variable}=logfunction^{\prime}(0)=realparam
\]

Applying the continuous function \( e^{variable} \) yields
\[
\lim _{variable \rightarrow 0}(1+realparam\ variable)^{1 / variable}=e^{realparam}
\]
and taking the sequence \( variable_{n}=1 / 2^{n} \) yields
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{realparam}{2^{n}}\right)^{2^{n}}=e^{realparam}
\]
so \( \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} sequencelimit(n)=e^{realparam}-1 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $seesawing$ be a real number. For each integer $lighthouse \geq 0$, define a
sequence $\{pendulum(windchime)\}$, $windchime=0,1,2,\dots$ by the condition
\begin{align*}
pendulum(0) &= seesawing/2^{lighthouse}, \\
pendulum(windchime+1) &= (pendulum(windchime))^2 + 2pendulum(windchime), \qquad windchime \geq 0.
\end{align*}
Evaluate $\lim_{n \to \infty} periscope(n)$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. We have \( pendulum(windchime+1)+1=\left(pendulum(windchime)+1\right)^{2} \), so by induction on \( windchime \),
\[
pendulum(windchime)+1=\left(pendulum(0)+1\right)^{2^{windchime}} .
\]

Hence
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} periscope(n)=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{seesawing}{2^{n}}\right)^{2^{n}}-1
\]

If \( quagmire(bottleneck)=\ln (1+seesawing\, bottleneck) \), then \( quagmire^{\prime}(bottleneck)=seesawing /(1+seesawing\, bottleneck) \), and in particular
\[
\lim _{bottleneck \rightarrow 0} \frac{\ln (1+seesawing\, bottleneck)}{bottleneck}=quagmire^{\prime}(0)=seesawing
\]

Applying the continuous function \( e^{bottleneck} \) yields
\[
\lim _{bottleneck \rightarrow 0}(1+seesawing\, bottleneck)^{1 / bottleneck}=e^{seesawing}
\]
and taking the sequence \( bottleneck_{n}=1 / 2^{n} \) yields
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{seesawing}{2^{n}}\right)^{2^{n}}=e^{seesawing}
\]
so \( \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} periscope(n)=e^{seesawing}-1 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $variability$ be a real number. For each integer $continuum \geq 0$, define a
sequence $\{giganticconstant_{continuum}(staticity)\}$, $staticity=0,1,2,\dots$ by the condition
\begin{align*}
giganticconstant_{continuum}(0) &= variability/2^{continuum}, \\
giganticconstant_{continuum}(staticity+1) &= (giganticconstant_{continuum}(staticity))^2 + 2giganticconstant_{continuum}(staticity), \qquad staticity \geq 0.
\end{align*}
Evaluate $\lim_{n \to \infty} minusculevariable_{n}(n)$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. We have \( giganticconstant_{\continuum}(staticity+1)+1=\left(giganticconstant_{\continuum}(staticity)+1\right)^{2} \), so by induction on \( staticity \),
\[
giganticconstant_{\continuum}(staticity)+1=\left(giganticconstant_{\continuum}(0)+1\right)^{2^{staticity}} .
\]

Hence
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} minusculevariable_{n}(n)=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{variability}{2^{n}}\right)^{2^{n}}-1
\]

If \( nonfunction(unknownless)=\ln (1+variability\, unknownless) \), then \( nonfunction^{\prime}(unknownless)=variability /(1+variability\, unknownless) \), and in particular
\[
\lim _{unknownless \rightarrow 0} \frac{\ln (1+variability\, unknownless)}{unknownless}=nonfunction^{\prime}(0)=variability
\]

Applying the continuous function \( e^{unknownless} \) yields
\[
\lim _{unknownless \rightarrow 0}(1+variability\, unknownless)^{1 / unknownless}=e^{variability}
\]
and taking the sequence \( unknownless_{n}=1 / 2^{n} \) yields
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{variability}{2^{n}}\right)^{2^{n}}=e^{variability}
\]
so \( \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} minusculevariable_{n}(n)=e^{variability}-1 \).

garbled_string.question:
Let $cgevfrat$ be a real number. For each integer $qzxwvtnp \geq 0$, define a
sequence $\{flkseowq(hjgrksla)\}$, $hjgrksla=0,1,2,\dots$ by the condition
\begin{align*}
flkseowq(0) &= cgevfrat/2^{qzxwvtnp}, \\
flkseowq(hjgrksla+1) &= (flkseowq(hjgrksla))^2 + 2flkseowq(hjgrksla), \qquad hjgrksla \geq 0.
\end{align*}
Evaluate $\lim_{n \to \infty} nsdijcka(n)$.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. We have \( flkseowq(hjgrksla+1)+1=\left(flkseowq(hjgrksla)+1\right)^{2} \), so by induction on \( hjgrksla \),
\[
flkseowq(hjgrksla)+1=\left(flkseowq(0)+1\right)^{2^{hjgrksla}} .
\]

Hence
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} nsdijcka(n)=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{cgevfrat}{2^{n}}\right)^{2^{n}}-1
\]

If \( prtwyvqm(udmnpyla)=\ln (1+cgevfrat \, udmnpyla) \), then \( prtwyvqm^{\prime}(udmnpyla)=cgevfrat /(1+cgevfrat \, udmnpyla) \), and in particular
\[
\lim _{udmnpyla \rightarrow 0} \frac{\ln (1+cgevfrat \, udmnpyla)}{udmnpyla}=prtwyvqm^{\prime}(0)=cgevfrat
\]

Applying the continuous function \( e^{udmnpyla} \) yields
\[
\lim _{udmnpyla \rightarrow 0}(1+cgevfrat \, udmnpyla)^{1 / udmnpyla}=e^{cgevfrat}
\]
and taking the sequence \( udmnpyla_{n}=1 / 2^{n} \) yields
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{cgevfrat}{2^{n}}\right)^{2^{n}}=e^{cgevfrat}
\]
so \( \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} nsdijcka(n)=e^{cgevfrat}-1 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let d be a real number.  
For every integer m \geq  1 define a sequence {a_m(j)}_{j=0}^{\infty } recursively by  

 a_m(0) = d / m!,  

 a_m(j+1) = (a_m(j))^{\,j+1} + (j+1)(a_m(j))^{\,j} + C(j+1,2)(a_m(j))^{\,j-1} + \ldots  + (j+1)a_m(j), (j \geq  0),  

where C(j+1,k) denotes the usual binomial coefficients.  

(Equivalently, a_m(j+1) = (1 + a_m(j))^{\,j+1} - 1.)  

Compute simultaneously  

(A) L = lim_{n\to \infty } a_n(n),  

(B) lim_{n\to \infty } n! \cdot  (a_n(n) - L).
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1. A convenient substitution.  
Put b_m(j) := a_m(j)+1.  
The recurrence becomes
 b_m(j+1) = b_m(j)^{\,j+1}, b_m(0) = 1 + d/m!.

Step 2. Closed form for b_m(j).  
We claim b_m(j) = (1 + d/m!)^{j!}.  
Proof by induction on j.  
- Base j = 0: b_m(0) = 1 + d/m! = (1 + d/m!)^{0!}.  
- Inductive step: assume b_m(j) = (1 + d/m!)^{j!}.  
Then  
 b_m(j+1) = b_m(j)^{\,j+1} = (1 + d/m!)^{j!(j+1)} = (1 + d/m!)^{(j+1)!},  
completing the induction.

Hence  
 a_m(j) = b_m(j) - 1 = (1 + d/m!)^{j!} - 1.

Step 3. Exact expression for the quantity of interest.  
With j = m we get  
 a_m(m) = (1 + d/m!)^{m!} - 1.              (\star )

Part (A). Limit of a_n(n).  
Write y_n = d/n!.  Then (\star ) reads a_n(n) = (1 + y_n)^{1/y_n\cdot d} - 1 with y_n \to  0.  
Because  
 lim_{x\to 0} (1+x)^{1/x} = e,  
we obtain  
 lim_{n\to \infty } a_n(n) = e^{d} - 1.  
Therefore  
 L = e^{d} - 1.

Part (B). First-order asymptotics around the limit.  
We need n!\cdot (a_n(n) - (e^{d} - 1)).  
Start again from (\star ):

ln(1 + a_n(n)) = n!\cdot ln(1 + d/n!)                         (1)

Expand ln(1 + z) for small z: ln(1 + z) = z - z^2/2 + z^3/3 - \ldots   
Insert z = d/n!:

ln(1 + a_n(n)) = n!\cdot ( d/n! - d^2/(2 n!^2) + O(1/n!^3) )
              = d - d^2/(2 n!) + O(1/n!^2).             (2)

Exponentiating (2):

1 + a_n(n) = exp(d - d^2/(2 n!) + O(1/n!^2))
          = e^{d}\cdot exp(-d^2/(2 n!) + O(1/n!^2))
          = e^{d}\cdot (1 - d^2/(2 n!) + O(1/n!^2)).          (3)

Thus

a_n(n) = e^{d} - 1 - e^{d}\cdot d^2/(2 n!) + O(1/n!^2).        (4)

Multiplying by n! gives

n!\cdot (a_n(n) - (e^{d} - 1)) = -e^{d}\cdot d^2/2 + O(1/n!).      (5)

Taking the limit n\to \infty  we obtain the exact constant

lim_{n\to \infty } n!\cdot (a_n(n) - L) = -\frac{1}{2} d^2 e^{d}.                (Answer to B)

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original problem (and the current kernel variant), this enhanced version is substantially harder for several reasons:

1. Variable-degree iteration: the exponent in each step is j+1, so after m steps a factorial exponent m! appears. Tracking this quickly-growing, non-constant exponent requires a careful inductive argument and is no longer visible from a single pattern.

2. Factorial-scale starting value: the initial term d/m! is delicately tuned to the factorial growth of the exponent; recognising this tuning and its consequences demands deeper insight (and familiarity with limits of the form (1+x)^{x^{-1}}).

3. Two-part limit: besides finding the main limit (analogous to the original task), one must extract the first-order error term. This forces the solver to employ a second-order Taylor expansion of ln(1+x) and to control the error terms precisely—techniques completely absent from the original solution.

4. Asymptotic analysis with factorials: the presence of n! in both base and exponent obliges the solver to manipulate logarithmic expansions and exponential re-expansions at factorial scales, far beyond the elementary “substitute 1/2^{n}” step in the original.

5. Sign determination and constant identification: the exact constant −½ d² e^{d} in Part (B) cannot be guessed; it emerges only after a meticulous asymptotic computation.

Together, these additions raise the technical level from a single-observation exercise to a multi-stage argument involving induction, limit theorems, Taylor expansions, and asymptotic control.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let d be a real number.  
For every integer m \geq  1 define a sequence {a_m(j)}_{j=0}^{\infty } recursively by  

 a_m(0) = d / m!,  

 a_m(j+1) = (a_m(j))^{\,j+1} + (j+1)(a_m(j))^{\,j} + C(j+1,2)(a_m(j))^{\,j-1} + \ldots  + (j+1)a_m(j), (j \geq  0),  

where C(j+1,k) denotes the usual binomial coefficients.  

(Equivalently, a_m(j+1) = (1 + a_m(j))^{\,j+1} - 1.)

(A) L = lim_{n\to \infty } a_n(n),  

(B) lim_{n\to \infty } n! \cdot  (a_n(n) - L).

The recurrence becomes

Step 3. Exact expression for the quantity of interest.

Part (A). Limit of a_n(n).

ln(1 + a_n(n)) = n!\cdot ln(1 + d/n!)                         (1)

Expand ln(1 + z) for small z: ln(1 + z) = z - z^2/2 + z^3/3 - \ldots   
Insert z = d/n!:

ln(1 + a_n(n)) = n!\cdot ( d/n! - d^2/(2 n!^2) + O(1/n!^3) )
              = d - d^2/(2 n!) + O(1/n!^2).             (2)

Exponentiating (2):

1 + a_n(n) = exp(d - d^2/(2 n!) + O(1/n!^2))
          = e^{d}\cdot exp(-d^2/(2 n!) + O(1/n!^2))
          = e^{d}\cdot (1 - d^2/(2 n!) + O(1/n!^2)).          (3)

Thus

a_n(n) = e^{d} - 1 - e^{d}\cdot d^2/(2 n!) + O(1/n!^2).        (4)

Multiplying by n! gives

n!\cdot (a_n(n) - (e^{d} - 1)) = -e^{d}\cdot d^2/2 + O(1/n!).      (5)

Taking the limit n\to \infty  we obtain the exact constant

lim_{n\to \infty } n!\cdot (a_n(n) - L) = -\frac{1}{2} d^2 e^{d}.                (Answer to B)
